metadata.title: Criteria Before Output: Know What Done Looks Like
metadata.description_blurb: The grant writer who didn't get funded had clean prose and wrong criteria — she never told Claude, or herself, what 'ready to submit' actually meant.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: The grant proposal was rejected. The prose was clean. The structure was correct. The sections were complete. What was missing was not in the output — it was never in the prompt. The need statement used the wrong poverty-rate figures. The evaluation plan had no measurable indicators. The budget narrative did not match the project description. She had not told Claude — or herself — how she would know the draft was ready to submit. This is Liam, in for Bear.
beats.new_visual_element: ClaudeComposerAsk — cold open, draft the grant proposal

props.command: draft a grant proposal for this community health program

beats.narration_text: Four types of evaluation criteria. Accuracy criteria: what the output must get right — every factual claim traceable to a source. Completeness criteria: what the output must include — each program component addressed, each recommendation accompanied by a rationale. Format criteria: what the output must look like — under five hundred words, three sections. Failure criteria: conditions that make the output wrong regardless of everything else — if any statistic cannot be traced to provided data, the output fails.
beats.new_visual_element: Four criteria types — accuracy / completeness / format / failure, each with example

beats.narration_text: Failure criteria are the most powerful and most skipped. A failure criterion names the condition that makes the output wrong regardless of its other merits — 'if the tone shifts from neutral to advocate, the output fails,' 'if any recommendation is not supported by the evidence, the output fails.' The power: they define the catastrophic before generation. The output cannot accidentally pass a failure criterion you named before it was written.
beats.new_visual_element: Failure criteria — define catastrophic before generation / output cannot accidentally pass them

beats.narration_text: Writing evaluation criteria before generation reveals requirements you had not consciously articulated. The act of writing 'every measurable indicator must have a baseline and a target year' forces the question: do I know what the baseline is? Writing 'the budget narrative must match the project description line by line' forces the question: do I have the budget? The discovery is valuable on its own — it is the Gru work that would have happened in the review step, moved to before the draft.
beats.new_visual_element: Criteria reveal requirements — writing criteria surfaces unstated assumptions before generation

beats.narration_text: Criteria-first prompting does not demand perfection. It makes the task inspectable. Claude uses the criteria as part of the specification. You use them as the review checklist. The criteria make the review faster — you have a list of exactly what to check. And the failure criteria make it possible to fail the output before it goes anywhere. A draft that passes all stated criteria is auditable. A draft with no stated criteria is interpretable. Those are different products.
beats.new_visual_element: Artifact view — criteria-first: auditable output / no criteria: interpretable output

beats.narration_text: Write your evaluation criteria before your next generation. Paste this.

props.command: Before generating anything, help me write evaluation criteria for this task: [describe it]. For each type, give me specific checkable conditions: (1) accuracy criteria — what must be traceable to provided sources, (2) completeness criteria — what must be included, (3) format criteria — what the output must look like, (4) failure criteria — conditions that make the output wrong regardless of everything else. Then generate the output using these criteria as the specification.

beats.narration_text: If this saves you from reviewing a draft with criteria you never named, subscribe. Liam, in for Bear.
